summary:
The Phoenicians were a Semitic-speaking people of the Levantine coast, centered on a string of city-states including Byblos, Sidon, and Tyre, who rose to commercial and maritime prominence from around 1500 BC onward. They were not a unified state but a culturally coherent civilization bound together by shared language, religion, and trade networks rather than political unity.

Phoenicia occupied a narrow coastal strip between the Lebanon Mountains and the Mediterranean, with limited agricultural land but excellent harbors, skilled craftsmen, and access to the famous cedars of Lebanon — a prized timber exported to Egypt and Mesopotamia for millennia. Phoenician artisans produced luxury goods highly valued throughout the ancient world, including purple-dyed textiles (the color extracted from Murex sea snails), carved ivory, blown glass, and metalwork. Trade in these goods financed the construction of impressive urban centers and funded colonial ventures.

The most consequential Phoenician contribution to world civilization was the [[event:1835|Phoenician alphabet]], developed around 1050 BC. This consonantal alphabet of twenty-two characters replaced the far more complex cuneiform and hieroglyphic writing systems for commercial correspondence and eventually served as the ancestor of Greek, Latin, Arabic, Hebrew, and most other alphabets in use today. Earlier developments such as the [[event:1834|Ugaritic alphabet]] prepared the ground for this achievement.

Phoenician maritime reach was extraordinary for the ancient world. By the ninth century BC, Phoenician sailors had established trading colonies across the western Mediterranean, including Carthage (traditionally founded 814 BC), Cadiz, and settlements in Cyprus, Sardinia, and Malta. Ancient accounts even describe Phoenician mariners circumnavigating Africa at the commission of an Egyptian pharaoh. This maritime activity placed Phoenicia at the center of Mediterranean commerce for centuries, until competition from Greek colonists and eventual conquest by Persia and later Alexander diminished Phoenician independence.
